section.title: Healing at Bethesda and the Authority of the Son
section.sectionRationale: The third sign, a man healed at the pool of Bethesda after thirty-eight years, sparks the Gospel's first open conflict. Because Jesus healed on the Sabbath and called God His own Father, making Himself equal with God, the authorities seek to kill Him. Jesus responds with a sweeping discourse on His unity with the Father: the Son gives life and executes judgment, and a chorus of witnesses, John, the works, the Father, and the Scriptures, testify that He is sent from God.
section.thesis: The Son does only what He sees the Father doing, and the Father has granted Him to give life to the dead and to execute all judgment, so that all must honor the Son as they honor the Father, and a fourfold witness, John, the works, the Father, and the Scriptures, confirms that to refuse the Son is to refuse life itself.
section.frameNote: Three movements: the healing at Bethesda and the Sabbath conflict; the discourse on the Son's authority to give life and to judge in perfect union with the Father; and the fourfold testimony, John, the works, the Father, and the Scriptures, that indicts unbelief.

units.label: The pool of Bethesda
units.note: do you want to get well

verses.text: Some time later there was a feast of the Jews, and Jesus went up to Jerusalem.
Now there is in Jerusalem near the Sheep Gate a pool with five covered colonnades, which in Hebrew is called Bethesda.
On these walkways lay a great number of the sick, the blind, the lame, and the paralyzed.
One man there had been an invalid for thirty-eight years.
When Jesus saw him lying there and realized that he had spent a long time in this condition, He asked him, “Do you want to get well?”
“Sir,” the invalid replied, “I have no one to help me into the pool when the water is stirred. While I am on my way, someone else goes in before me.”
Then Jesus told him, “Get up, pick up your mat, and walk.”
Immediately the man was made well, and he picked up his mat and began to walk. Now this happened on the Sabbath day,
so the Jews said to the man who had been healed, “This is the Sabbath! It is unlawful for you to carry your mat.”
But he answered, “The man who made me well told me, ‘Pick up your mat and walk.’”
“Who is this man who told you to pick it up and walk?” they asked.
But the man who was healed did not know who it was, for Jesus had slipped away while the crowd was there.
Afterward, Jesus found the man at the temple and said to him, “See, you have been made well. Stop sinning, or something worse may happen to you.”
And the man went away and told the Jews that it was Jesus who had made him well.

units.label: The Father and the Son
units.note: the Son gives life and executes judgment

verses.text: Now because Jesus was doing these things on the Sabbath, the Jews began to persecute Him.
But Jesus answered them, “To this very day My Father is at His work, and I too am working.”
Because of this, the Jews tried all the harder to kill Him. Not only was He breaking the Sabbath, but He was even calling God His own Father, making Himself equal with God.
So Jesus replied, “Truly, truly, I tell you, the Son can do nothing by Himself, unless He sees the Father doing it. For whatever the Father does, the Son also does.
The Father loves the Son and shows Him all He does. And to your amazement, He will show Him even greater works than these.
For just as the Father raises the dead and gives them life, so also the Son gives {{zoe|life}} to whom He wishes.
Furthermore, the Father judges no one, but has assigned all {{krisis|judgment}} to the Son,
so that all may honor the Son just as they honor the Father. Whoever does not honor the Son does not honor the Father who sent Him.
Truly, truly, I tell you, whoever hears My word and believes Him who sent Me has eternal life and will not come under judgment. Indeed, he has crossed over from death to life.
Truly, truly, I tell you, the hour is coming and has now come when the dead will hear the voice of the Son of God, and those who hear will live.
For as the Father has life in Himself, so also He has granted the Son to have life in Himself.
And He has given Him authority to execute judgment, because He is the Son of Man.
Do not be amazed at this, for the hour is coming when all who are in their graves will hear His voice
and come out, those who have done good to the resurrection of life, and those who have done evil to the resurrection of judgment.
I can do nothing by Myself; I judge only as I hear. And My judgment is just, because I do not seek My own will, but the will of Him who sent Me.

units.label: Testimonies about Jesus
units.note: John, the works, the Father, and the Scriptures

verses.text: If I testify about Myself, My {{martyria|testimony}} is not valid.
There is another who testifies about Me, and I know that His testimony about Me is valid.
You have sent to John, and he has testified to the truth.
Even though I do not accept human testimony, I say these things so that you may be saved.
John was a lamp that burned and gave light, and you were willing for a season to bask in his light.
But I have testimony more substantial than that of John. For the {{ergon|works}} that the Father has given Me to accomplish, the very works I am doing, testify about Me that the Father has sent Me.
And the Father who sent Me has Himself testified about Me. You have never heard His voice nor seen His form,
nor does His word abide in you, because you do not believe the One He sent.
You pore over the Scriptures because you presume that by them you possess eternal life. These are the very words that testify about Me,
yet you refuse to come to Me to have life.
I do not accept glory from men,
but I know you, that you do not have the love of God within you.
I have come in My Father’s name, and you have not received Me; but if someone else comes in his own name, you will receive him.
How can you believe if you accept glory from one another, yet do not seek the glory that comes from the only God?
Do not think that I will accuse you before the Father. Your accuser is Moses, in whom you have put your hope.
If you had believed Moses, you would believe Me, because he wrote about Me.
But since you do not believe what he wrote, how will you believe what I say?”

zoe.gloss: Life, and in John especially the life of God, eternal life. The Father has life in Himself and has granted the Son to have life in Himself, so that the Son gives life to whom He wishes. This life is received now by hearing Christ's word and believing, a crossing over from death to life, and it will be consummated in the resurrection of the body.

krisis.gloss: Judgment, the act of judging and the verdict it renders. The Father has assigned all judgment to the Son, giving Him authority to judge because He is the Son of Man. Judgment is both present, as people respond to His word, and future, at the resurrection to life or to judgment. To honor or dishonor the Son is to settle one's standing before the Judge of all.

martyria.gloss: Testimony, witness, legal evidence. Jesus does not stand on His own unsupported word but marshals a fourfold witness: John the Baptist, the works the Father gave Him to do, the Father Himself, and the Scriptures. In a case where two or three witnesses establish a matter, the testimony to Jesus is overwhelming, so unbelief is shown to be culpable, not reasonable.

ergon.gloss: Work, deed. The Father is always working, even on the Sabbath, and the Son works with Him. The works the Father gave Jesus to accomplish, the signs and ultimately the cross, are themselves a testimony that the Father has sent Him. In John the works reveal the worker; to see the works rightly is to recognize the Son.

basic.context: At a feast in Jerusalem, Jesus heals a man who had been an invalid for thirty-eight years, telling him to pick up his mat and walk. Because it was the Sabbath, the authorities object, and when they learn Jesus did it, and hear Him call God His own Father, they seek to kill Him for making Himself equal with God. Jesus does not soften the claim; He explains it. The Son does only what He sees the Father doing, and the Father has given Him to give life to the dead and to judge all people. He then calls four witnesses to back His claim: John the Baptist, His works, the Father, and the Scriptures.
basic.hermeneutics: The key issue is the identity of Jesus. His enemies rightly understood that calling God His own Father meant claiming equality with God; the question is whether the claim is true. Jesus answers not by retreating but by describing a unique unity with the Father, in work, in giving life, and in judgment. The key questions are, does the Son truly share the Father's authority, and will we honor Him accordingly. The chapter warns that even diligent Bible study is worthless if it does not lead us to come to Christ for life.
basic.originalLanguages: The discourse gathers key words. The Son gives zoe, the very life of God, to whom He wishes. The Father has assigned all krisis, judgment, to the Son. Jesus grounds His claim in a fourfold martyria, testimony: John, the works, the Father, and the Scriptures. And the ergon, the works the Father gave Him, themselves testify that the Father has sent Him.
basic.typology: The Sabbath itself points forward: the Father works to sustain and save even on the Sabbath, and the Son brings the true rest of restored life. Moses, whom the leaders trust, wrote about Christ, so the Scriptures are a signpost to Him. The resurrection to life fulfills the hope of Daniel 12.
basic.christ: Christ claims to share the Father's very life and authority: He gives life to the dead and judges all people. He is to be honored exactly as the Father is honored. He is the Son of Man to whom judgment is entrusted, and the one to whom all the Scriptures point.
basic.god: God is the Father who is always working, who loves the Son and shows Him all He does, and who has given the Son to give life and to judge, so that the Father is honored in the Son. The Father Himself testifies to the Son, and refusing the Son dishonors the Father.
basic.crossReferences: Daniel 12 speaks of resurrection to life and to shame. Deuteronomy 19 requires two or three witnesses. Luke 24 shows Christ opening the Scriptures that testify about Him. John 1 said the Word was God, now claimed openly.
basic.oneStory: The one who heals a lifelong invalid claims to share the Father's authority to give life and to judge. The Scriptures the leaders revere, and Moses in whom they hope, all point to Him. The story of God's saving work narrows to the Son who gives life to the dead and before whom all will finally stand.

context.literary: A sign followed by a discourse that interprets it, the pattern of the Book of Signs. The healing raises the Sabbath conflict, which raises the question of Jesus' authority, which Jesus answers with the longest sustained Christological claim so far. The discourse divides into the Son's shared work of giving life and judging, and then a legal argument marshaling witnesses, ending with the indictment that Moses accuses those who reject the one he wrote about.
context.historical: The pool of Bethesda near the Sheep Gate has been identified archaeologically, with porticoes matching the description. Sabbath law, elaborated in oral tradition, forbade carrying burdens, so the healed man's mat becomes the flashpoint. To call God one's own Father in this exclusive way, and to work as the Father works, was heard as a claim to deity, which is why the charge escalates to a capital one.
context.canonical: The discourse stands with the New Testament's clearest claims to the Son's deity and His shared work of creation, life-giving, and judgment. The resurrection of the just and the unjust echoes Daniel 12:2 and anticipates Acts 24:15 and Revelation 20. The insistence that the Scriptures testify about Christ frames the whole Bible as pointing to Him, as Luke 24:27 and 44 also teach.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To present the healing as a sign that provokes the question of Jesus' identity, and to record His own answer: that He shares the Father's authority to give life and to judge, supported by a fourfold witness, so that readers will honor the Son and come to Him for life.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The narrative describes a particular healing and confrontation. Its abiding claims are prescriptive: the Son must be honored as the Father is honored, life comes only by hearing His word and believing, and the Scriptures are to be read as testimony to Christ rather than as an end in themselves.

debates.question: What did Jesus mean by My Father is always working, and I too am working, on the Sabbath?

views.label: God never ceases His sustaining and saving work
views.case: Rabbis recognized that God upholds the world and gives and takes life even on the Sabbath; Jesus claims the same divine prerogative, working as the Father works, which is why the leaders hear a claim to deity.

views.label: The Sabbath points to redemptive rest now dawning
views.case: Jesus' Sabbath healings signal the arrival of the true rest and restoration, so that His work fulfills rather than violates the Sabbath's purpose.

debates.bearing: Both underline the same claim: Jesus works with the very authority of God, so the Sabbath objection actually exposes who He is, the Son who does what only God does.

debates.question: Is the judgment and resurrection Jesus speaks of present or future?

views.label: Already present in His word
views.case: Verses 24 and 25 say the hour has now come; those who hear and believe have already crossed from death to life, a present, spiritual resurrection through Christ's word.

views.label: Still future at the last day
views.case: Verses 28 and 29 point to the hour coming when all in the graves will hear His voice and rise to life or judgment, a future, bodily resurrection.

debates.bearing: John holds both together: the life of the age to come is received now by faith, and it will be completed in the bodily resurrection, all through the voice of the Son.

study.originalLanguages: The discourse is dense with key terms. The Son gives zoe, the very life of God, to whom He wishes, because the Father has granted Him to have life in Himself. The Father has assigned all krisis, judgment, to the Son, giving Him authority to judge because He is the Son of Man. When challenged, Jesus does not rest on His own word alone but calls a fourfold martyria, testimony: John the Baptist, His ergon, the works the Father gave Him to accomplish, the Father Himself, and the Scriptures that testify about Him. The weight of witness is overwhelming, so that unbelief is not for lack of evidence but for lack of the love of God within.

typology.fulfillment: Jesus, the Son who works as the Father works, brings the true rest and restoration the Sabbath foreshadowed, John 5:17 and Hebrews 4:9-10.
The hour when all in the graves hear the Son's voice and rise to life or to judgment, John 5:28-29.
The writings of Moses and the whole of Scripture testify about Christ and find their meaning in Him, John 5:39 and 46.

study.christ: Christ makes some of His highest claims here. He works as the Father works, so that His enemies rightly see Him claiming equality with God. He does only what He sees the Father doing, in perfect unity, not rivalry. He gives life to the dead, both now through His word and finally in the resurrection, and He has been given all authority to judge because He is the Son of Man. He is to be honored exactly as the Father is honored, so that to dishonor the Son is to dishonor the Father. And He is the one to whom John, the works, the Father, and the Scriptures all bear witness.
study.god: The Father is revealed as ceaselessly at work, sustaining and saving even on the Sabbath, and as loving the Son and showing Him all that He does. He has granted the Son to have life in Himself and has entrusted all judgment to Him, precisely so that the Son may be honored as the Father is honored. The Father Himself bears witness to the Son, and He seeks a people who love Him and will come to His Son for life. To refuse the Son is to refuse the Father who sent Him.

crossReferences.note: Many who sleep in the dust will awake, some to life and some to shame, behind the resurrection of life and of judgment.
A matter is established by two or three witnesses, the legal principle behind Jesus' marshaling of testimony.
Beginning with Moses and all the Prophets, Jesus explained what the Scriptures said about Himself.
A Sabbath rest remains for the people of God, the true rest Jesus brings.

study.oneStory: The man healed after thirty-eight years is a sign of something greater: the Son who gives life to the dead. Challenged for making Himself equal with God, Jesus explains rather than retracts, claiming to share the Father's authority to give life and to judge. The Scriptures the leaders study, and Moses in whom they hope, all point to Him. The whole story of God's saving work draws to a single center, the Son who must be honored as the Father, who gives life now to all who hear His word, and before whom all the dead will one day rise.

quiz.q: Why did the authorities seek to kill Jesus after the healing at Bethesda?

options.text: Because He healed on the Sabbath and called God His own Father, making Himself equal with God.
options.note: Verse 18 gives both reasons, the Sabbath and the claim to deity.

options.text: Only because He broke the Sabbath by healing.
options.note: The Sabbath was one reason, but the deeper offense was His claim to be equal with God.

options.text: Because He refused to heal anyone else at the pool.
options.note: The issue was not whom He healed but the Sabbath and His claim about the Father.

options.text: Because He told the man to stop sinning.
options.note: His words to the man are not the reason given; the conflict was over the Sabbath and His equality with God.

quiz.q: What authority does Jesus claim to share with the Father?

options.text: To give life to the dead and to execute all judgment.
options.note: Verses 21 to 27 assign both life-giving and judgment to the Son.

options.text: Only the authority to heal the sick.
options.note: Healing is a sign, but Jesus claims far more, to give life and to judge all.

options.text: The authority to give eternal life to those who believe.
options.note: True and central, but it omits the equally emphasized authority to judge all people.

options.text: No real authority of His own, only the power to teach.
options.note: Jesus claims to do whatever the Father does, sharing His authority, not merely to teach.

quiz.q: How should people respond to the Son, according to verse 23?

options.text: They must honor the Son just as they honor the Father.
options.note: Verse 23 makes honoring the Son the measure of honoring the Father.

options.text: They should respect Him as one prophet among many.
options.note: He is to be honored as the Father is honored, far above a prophet.

options.text: They should admire His teaching and good works.
options.note: Admiration falls short of the honor due to the Son, which equals the honor due the Father.

options.text: They should give Him the honor due to the Father who sent Him.
options.note: True and close, but it slightly understates that the Son is honored just as the Father is.

quiz.q: What witnesses does Jesus call to testify that He is sent from God?

options.text: John the Baptist, His works, the Father, and the Scriptures.
options.note: Verses 33 to 39 marshal all four witnesses to Jesus.

options.text: Only John the Baptist.
options.note: John is one witness, but Jesus says his own works are a greater testimony, along with the Father and the Scriptures.

options.text: His works and the Scriptures.
options.note: Both are named, but the list leaves out John the Baptist and the Father's own testimony.

options.text: The crowds who saw the healing.
options.note: Jesus does not appeal to the crowd's testimony; He names John, the works, the Father, and the Scriptures.

quiz.q: What does Jesus say about the Scriptures the leaders study so diligently?

options.text: They testify about Him, yet the leaders refuse to come to Him for life.
options.note: Verses 39 and 40 say the Scriptures point to Christ, but the leaders will not come to Him.

options.text: They are unnecessary now that Jesus has come.
options.note: Jesus affirms the Scriptures; the problem is failing to see that they point to Him.

options.text: They contain eternal life apart from Christ.
options.note: The leaders presumed this, but Jesus says life is found in coming to Him, to whom the Scriptures point.

options.text: They bear witness to Christ, and Moses wrote about Him.
options.note: True, but it omits the sharp indictment that they refuse to come to Him to have life.

questions: Jesus asked the invalid, do you want to get well. Where has a long struggle left you resigned, and how might Jesus be asking whether you truly want His healing?
The Son does only what He sees the Father doing, in perfect unity. How does this picture of the Son and the Father deepen your worship of Jesus?
Jesus said whoever hears His word and believes has already crossed from death to life. Do you rest in that present assurance, or do you live as if life is still uncertain?
The leaders searched the Scriptures yet would not come to Christ for life. How can you keep your Bible reading from becoming an end in itself rather than a way to meet Jesus?
Jesus said they sought glory from one another rather than the glory that comes from God. Whose approval are you living for, and how does that shape your faith?

translationNotes.note: Bethesda; the BSB footnote notes that Hebrew may mean Aramaic, and that some manuscripts read Bethzatha. The pool near the Sheep Gate has been located by archaeologists.

The BSB prints verse 3 and moves directly to verse 5, because the words about an angel stirring the water, traditionally numbered verse 4, are absent from the earliest manuscripts. The BSB footnote records the longer reading; it is a later explanatory addition, not part of the original text.
Authority to execute judgment because He is the Son of Man draws on Daniel 7:13-14, where the Son of Man is given dominion and judgment.
The resurrection of life and the resurrection of judgment; the BSB footnote points to Daniel 12:2, the Old Testament hope of a twofold resurrection.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for John 5:1-47. Verse text assembled by concatenating content segments in order; footnote markers and line breaks skipped. The BSB omits verse 4 (the angel stirring the water) as a later addition, so the verse list runs 3 then 5; this is noted in translationNotes. Em dashes in verses 29 and 36 were converted to commas. No em or en dashes remain. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon URL verified this session.
